title: J. Craig Venter 1946–2026 - Nature Biotechnology
text:
How does one go from ordinary beginnings to such extraordinary accomplishments? After Craig received the 2023 SynBioBeta Lifetime Achievement Award, I asked him directly, “How did you ever become Craig Venter?” He explained that growing up, he had no inclination to go into science, or even to go to college. He struggled in school but was a champion swimmer dreaming of a career in professional surfing. Here, he noted, was the seed of his future success: his struggles in school arose from a rare condition that left him without mental images. As he put it, inside his mind “it’s just dark!” This forced him to learn to think in broad, high-level concepts and primed him to pursue grand visions based on simple principles. He credited this way of thinking as the foundation for his future achievements. Continuing his story, he said his surfing ambitions came to an end when he was drafted into the Vietnam war. There, working as a medic, he was struck by how adrenaline allowed soldiers to keep fighting despite their injuries. The desire to understand this led him to obtain a PhD in brain physiology11 and embark on a scientific career.The next steps in ‘becoming Craig Venter’ came in the late 1980s, when he was cloning genes related to neurotransmitters. The automated DNA sequencer was introduced then, and many researchers acquired one, but they didn’t work well. Undeterred, Craig hacked his system, found a problem in the reagent concentrations, and re-formulated a reagent mix that worked. Suddenly, he was sequencing many more genes than anyone else12. This attracted venture capitalists, who were “begging him to take their money” to start a company to sequence genes for drug development. He declined, saying he wanted to stay in academia, but they persisted and he finally agreed — on condition that they would also fund him to start his own research institute! And so was born his first company, Human Genome Sciences, and his first research institute, The Institute for Genome Research. This highlighted what Craig considered the other key ingredients in his recipe for success: being a leader in new technology and realizing that private capital was a quicker route to building a research enterprise than government grants.
